The Hidden World of Deep-Sea Bioluminescence

By Dr. Marina Cortez, Marine Biologist

June 15, 2026

Below 200 metres, sunlight becomes a memory. Yet the deep ocean is far from dark: it pulses with cold, living light. Bioluminescence — the chemical production of light by living organisms — is the dominant mode of communication and predation in the bathypelagic zone, and researchers are only beginning to understand its full ecological role.

How the chemistry works

The reaction requires two compounds: a light-emitting molecule called luciferin and an enzyme called luciferase. When luciferin is oxidised in the presence of luciferase, the chemical energy is released as a photon rather than heat. The result is an extraordinarily efficient light source — roughly 90 % of the energy becomes light, compared with about 10 % for an incandescent bulb.

Different lineages have evolved the pathway independently at least 40 times. This convergent evolution suggests strong selective pressure: in an environment where every photon carries information, the ability to produce light is a decisive advantage.

Navigation and camouflage

Counter-illumination is among the most elegant uses of bioluminescence. Certain squids and sharks produce ventral light that matches the faint downwelling daylight from above, erasing their silhouette when viewed from below by a predator. The photophores responsible can adjust brightness in real time via a feedback loop involving upward-facing photoreceptors.

The burglar-alarm hypothesis

When a small crustacean is attacked, it may release a bioluminescent secretion that lights up its attacker, attracting secondary predators that redirect the threat. This indirect defence — named the burglar-alarm hypothesis by Edith Widder — has been demonstrated experimentally in several species of copepod and ostracod.

Research methods

Studying bioluminescence in situ is technically demanding. Conventional white-light remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) suppress the very signals researchers want to capture: the sudden illumination of an ROV silences nearby organisms for tens of minutes. The ORCA (Observing Reef and Canyon Animals) system addresses this by operating in near-total darkness, using red light (which most deep-sea species cannot perceive) and sensitive intensified cameras.

A 2025 survey of Monterey Canyon with ORCA found that bioluminescent events occurred in 86 % of sampled water columns at depths below 500 m. The authors estimate that bioluminescence is a near-universal phenomenon in the mesopelagic and bathypelagic zones — a revision of earlier estimates that placed the proportion closer to 40 %.

"We were lighting up the ocean when we thought we were observing it in the dark. The paradigm shift is not yet complete."

— Dr. Edith Widder, Ocean Research & Conservation Association

Implications for biotechnology

Luciferase reporter genes have been a staple of cell biology for decades, but the diversity of deep-sea luciferins is largely unexplored. Several start-ups are now cataloguing bathypelagic organisms specifically to identify novel luciferin-luciferase pairs with emission spectra better suited to tissue imaging, where conventional firefly luciferase's yellow-green peak is scattered and attenuated by haemoglobin absorption.
